coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanoparticles neutralize carbon dioxide (CO2) in cytotoxicity: Potent carbon emission induced resistance to anticancer nanomedicine and antibiotics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Shaimoldina, Ainur
Sergazina, Akbota
Myrzagali, Sandugash
Nazarbek, Guldan
Omarova, Zhuldyz
Mirza, Omar
Fan, Haiyan
Amin, Amr
Zhou, Weizhi
Xie, Yingqiu
coredata.dc:description: Excessive carbon emissions, especially CO2 release, have been a global concern. Few studies applied nanotechnology to relieve the ecotoxicity of CO2. Here, we applied carbon dots (CDs) to neutralize the CO2. We found CO2 induced the aggregation of CDs, which is of significance for CDs in enhanced fluorescence intensity but decreased CDs function in nanozyme activity, and reduced CDs toxicity to bacteria and cancer cells. Our data suggest the concern of CO2 release in global health in CDs mediated anticancer drug delivery and antibiotics resistance. However, enhanced fluorescence in cells which can be applied for bioimaging or CO2 sensing as simulated investigation by static charged attraction of positively charged CDs with negatively charged soluble HCO3 -. Thus, CO2 abrogates the nanomedicine efficacy in cancer cells and antibacterial and may induce drug resistance for patients undergoing chemotherapy or antibiotics therapy. To overcome the resistance, we may apply the CDs for a neutralization of CO2 for impact on anticancer nanomedicine and antibiotics and reducing the ecotoxicity in biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Nanomedicine
Carbon dioxide
Carbon neutralization
Cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots, chemical production of CO2, and commercial CO2 resources
SEM (scanning electron microscope), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and fluorescence spectra
Cell viability assay and confocal microscopy
Potential mechanisms of the association of CDs-CO2
Ethics approval and consent to participate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of beet-derived carbon dots (CDs) affected by CO2. SEM (A) and AFM (B) with particle size distributions were shown by images. C. FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-CO2. D. Modeling of the aggregation of CDs by CO2 induction.

CDs fluorescence enhancement by CO2. A UV-spectra of CDs with CO2. B. Fluorescence intensity (a.u.) measured indicates the enhancement by CO2 by different doses. C,D. Fluorescence intensity (a.u.) measured indicates the enhancement of CDs but not baked sugars by CO2 by reaction derived from NaHCO3 +HCl or CO2 water.

Nanozyme of phosphatase activities of CDs affected by CO2. A. Different pH effect. B. Enzyme kinetics. C. SEM of CDs-CO2 with BODIPY FL C5-ceramide in the cluster indicates the potent phosphatase association with phosphor-lipid.
CDs-CO2 function in antibacteria (A) and cytotoxicity in normal and cancer cells (B) and cellular fluorescence enhancement by CO2 by confocal microscopy investigation (C). C. Magnification: 60X.

CDs-CO2 exhibit positive charge and attraction of negatively charged HCO3
- by electrophoresis gel analysis (A) and modeling (B).

head.ce:title: Carbon nanoparticles neutralize carbon dioxide (CO2) in cytotoxicity: Potent carbon emission induced resistance to anticancer nanomedicine and antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of water-sediment regulation and eco-decontamination, Jinan, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive carbon emissions, especially CO2 release, have been a global concern. Few studies applied nanotechnology to relieve the ecotoxicity of CO2. Here, we applied carbon dots (CDs) to neutralize the CO2. We found CO2 induced the aggregation of CDs, which is of significance for CDs in enhanced fluorescence intensity but decreased CDs function in nanozyme activity, and reduced CDs toxicity to bacteria and cancer cells. Our data suggest the concern of CO2 release in global health in CDs mediated anticancer drug delivery and antibiotics resistance. However, enhanced fluorescence in cells which can be applied for bioimaging or CO2 sensing as simulated investigation by static charged attraction of positively charged CDs with negatively charged soluble HCO3 -. Thus, CO2 abrogates the nanomedicine efficacy in cancer cells and antibacterial and may induce drug resistance for patients undergoing chemotherapy or antibiotics therapy. To overcome the resistance, we may apply the CDs for a neutralization of CO2 for impact on anticancer nanomedicine and antibiotics and reducing the ecotoxicity in biological systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are recently reported particles, with a size at nanoscale, and wide applications in biosensing, imaging, and drug delivery (Chung et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2019; Wang and Lu, 2022) due to their essential properties such as electron transferability, fluorescence with high photoluminescent quantum yield (Arcudi et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019), high solubility in water, biocompatibility (Tian et al., 2020), environmental friendliness, chemical stability, and low or none toxicity (Ullal et al., 2022). The surface of CDs is rich for functional groups, providing significant sensor features with high selectivity. Moreover, depending on the application requirement, the physical and chemical properties of CDs can be adjusted through synthesis such as fluorescence (Mansuriya and Altintas, 2021). Previously, investigators tried to enhance the fluorescence of CDs by embedding fluorescent centers in several organic polymers, such as starch, and inorganic materials. For example, red-emissive CDs embedded with starch particles were examined for fingerprints (Dong et al., 2020), while CDs combined with mesoporous aluminas were used for sensing oxygen (He et al., 2017). In addition, the surface of carbon dots is applied to load organic groups for synthesis of nanozyme. Depending on the chemical composition of CDs and methods of synthesis, CDs can successfully act as nanozyme with hydrolase, phosphatase, peroxidase and oxidase like activities (Mi et al., 2018). The advantages of CD based nanozymes include higher chemical stability, low dependence on pH and temperature and, due to the ease of production, and lower cost.

Another application of CDs with high potential is inhibition of bacterial growth. Antimicrobial activity of CDs was based on several mechanisms such as destruction of biofilms, production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and interaction of bacteria with charged CDs (Li et al., 2018). Antimicrobial activity of carbon dots was explained by the presence of charged groups on their surface, which were also important in generation of ROS and weakened biofilm (Travlou et al., 2018). Moreover, organic groups on the surface of CD play a crucial role in antimicrobial activity as well (Jian et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020).

Bioimaging is taking advantage of fluorescent properties of CDs in cell or animal, human tissues in vivo. Imaging of lysosomes in living cells, by green fluorescent CDs with quantum yields as high as 90.49% were applied which takes 10 min for CDs to enter the cell and accumulate in lysosomes (Tong et al., 2020). Visualizations of CDs associated with biological complexes were explored and in vitro imaging showed high accumulation of red and near infrared CDs in the cytoplasm and plasma membrane of the cells (Yang et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2020). In this study, we made CDs from beet, and then examined their properties for sensing carbon dioxide (CO2) and how it affects CD, including photoluminescence, thereby bioimaging.

CO2 is one of the greenhouse gasses that cause climate change. Without considering the effect of the temperature increase on ecosystems and public health, high concentrations of atmospheric CO2 itself can be harmful. Short exposure of humans to CO2 beginning from 1000 ppm showed deteriorating effects on cognitive function and decision-making processes. Indoor exposure of children to higher than 1000 ppm concentration of carbon dioxide caused respiratory symptoms (Azuma et al., 2018). On the cellular level, high levels of CO2 can be toxic as well because its dissolved accumulation inhibits cell growth, impedes product formation, and causes alteration in the glycosylation pattern of proteins (Pattison et al., 2000).

The development of sensors is critical for the detection and monitoring of CO2 in the environment. Previous studies have been shown the quantum chemical simulation on a nanomaterial based on calcium oxide Ca12O12 for sensing not only CO2 but also SO2 and NO2. The results revealed that the adsorption of CO2 on calcium nanomaterials is stable (Louis et al., 2022). Another study developed a palladium-based nanocluster for carbon monoxide sensing. Investigators have found that the fluorescence emission of nanomaterial itself and nanomaterial bound to carbon monoxide are different, so that a change in the luminescence spectrum can be implicated in gas detection (Wang et al., 2019). However, CD-based CO2 nanosensors are few reported. Given that CDs application as a biosensor haven been widely studied, we aimed to test whether CDs can also serve as a CO2 sensor in biological systems such as cancer cells. Our hypothesis is that since CDs may have positive charge, water-soluble CO2 as HCO3 - may interact with CDs. Moreover, the associations may disrupt the functions of CDs in enzymatic, biocompatibility, anticancer and antibacterial functions. Thus, interplays between carbon nanoparticles, CO2, cytotoxicity, and their effectiveness on anti-cancer, anti-bacterial function will reveal the potential ecotoxicity and application of recovery. In this study, we developed CD sensors of CO2 based on their fluorescence spectrum shifts and investigated the complex morphology, ecotoxicity on cells and bacteria. We further applied the concept to reduce the ecotoxicity or overcome the CO2 mediated drug resistance to anti-cancer nanomedicine agent and antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots, chemical production of CO2, and commercial CO2 resources
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the carbon dots type of carbon nanoparticles were used. Synthesis of beet-derived CDs was reported previously (Xie et al., 2019). In brief, beet vegetable was soaked in 20 mL of deionized water (ddH2O) for 1 h and then stirred at 250 RPM on a hot plate at 60 °C for 2 h. The sample was mixed at the mass ratio of 1:4, respectively, with 60 mL of ddH2O and placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor, heated at 200 °C for 5 h, and filtered with filter paper, following by dialysis to remove larger sized particles. Finally the CDs were dissolved in ddH2O and with stock concentration of 50 mg/mL with sizes less than 200 nm. Commercial CO2 drinking water (TASSAY brand, Kazakhstan) was used as one type of CO2 resources. The other is produced from chemical reactions of 0.1 M NaHCO3 with 0.1 N HCl with equal molar concentration and volume for 10 min with fresh reaction when used. For controls, ddH2O or 0.1 M NaHCO3 alone was used for testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM (scanning electron microscope), fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and fluorescence spectra
ce:section.ce:para: For SEM, the steps of preparing samples include dilution (1:1000), sonication for 30 min with cycles of 10 s ON with amplitude of 20% and 30 s off. Finally, the sample of 4 μL CDs were dropped on the slide, air-dried, followed by SEM scanning. FT-IR was measured using beet-derived CDs and CO2 water. As described previously (Xie et al., 2019), the chemical groups of the samples were measured by Nicolet iS5, an FT-IR spectrometer with resolution of 2 cm− 1 (Thermo Scientific). The Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence absorbance of samples. An emission and excitation wavelength is 350 nm. The measurement was observed between the wavelength starting from 350 nm and stopping at 800 nm. The excitation and the emission slits are 20 nm for both of them.

The phosphatase enzyme activity analysis was carried out as manufacture’s manual using substrate of nitro-blue tetrazolium with 5-bromo-4-chloro-3′-indolyl phosphate (NBT/BCIP) (Thermo Scientific™) for reactions at room temperature for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 409 nm. The optimal concentration of substrates from the commercialized ready-to-use solution of 30 µl was used to measure the phosphatase activity at pH 7 or indicated by different pH, and different concentrations. For controls, ddH2O or 0.1 M NaHCO3 alone were used for testing.

Escherichia coli bacteria (DH5 alpha strain) were cultured in LB broth Lennox medium (Tryptone 10 g, Yeast extract 5 g, Sodium chloride 5 g), incubated in a shaking incubator overnight, and preliminary bacterial suspensions were obtained. Bacteria were treated by CDs-CO2 or ddH2O, which was used as a control for 2–3 h. After culturing them on the Petri dish, the growth was observed. The bacteria colonies formed were counted and recorded after incubating for 16 h at 37ºC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay and confocal microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Cell viability was measured after drug treatment of normal lung cells MRC5 or lung cancer A549 cells for 72–96 h in 24 well plate followed by crystal violet staining and optical density (OD) reading as described previously (Xie et al., 2019). Treatment includes beet-derived CD and CO2 water. In the testing, ddH2O was used as a control. For confocal microscopy, cells were grown overnight on a thin coverslip at 37 °C for 24 h. After the treatment with beet CDs, HCO3-, and their mix, following fixation by a 4% formaldehyde, blocking, staining with antibodies were performed overnight at 4 °C or 1 h at room temperature. Fluorescent imaging was captured at these parameters for channels: 405 nm, 488 nm, and 633 nm. For controls, ddH2O or 0.1 M NaHCO3 alone were used for testing.

Student's t-test was used to analyze the difference between experimental groups, with p-values less than 0.05 as statistically significant (*) and highly statistically significant with p-values less than 0.01 (**).

The physical characterization of produced CDs from beet was observed with SEM as shown in Fig. 1A, B. From Fig. 1A, beet CDs are separately round-shaped structures with a diameter of nearly 100 nm. However, after mixing with CO2 water, beet CDs clustered into groups forming a ring made of 5 to 15 nanoparticles (Fig. 1A). From this, it can be assumed that CO2 may affect the affinity of nanoparticles. This observation is consistent with the previous findings that various molecules can induce cluster formation of carbon dots and categorize them into different classes (Ru et al., 2022). For thickness, AFM images showed that CO2-doped CDs’ thickness may vary from 1 nm to 5 nm (Fig. 1B). In addition, FT-IR analysis showed the changes in surface groups of C-C-C and C-O upon CO2 bonding to CDs (Fig. 1C). Our data suggest that the CO2 affects the morphology, aggregation, and surface groups of CDs (Fig. 1D), which may affect CDs additional characteristics and functions such as anticancer and antibacteria.

Under irradiation of UV-light beet CDs can emit blue light. The luminescence of CDs-CO2 at different concentrations was measured by fluorescence spectroscopy using CO2 from two different sources, CO2 dissolved in water and CO2 produced from the neutralization reaction of NaHCO3 with HCl ( Fig. 2A, B,C). CDs-CO2 showed a CO2 concentration-dependent manner of enhanced intensity: the higher the concentration, the higher the intensity in reaction with CO2 generated from NaHCO3 with HCl. The CDs peak was near 450 nm while CDs-CO2 showed a shift to about 445 nm, by similar effect regardless of CO2 sources (Fig. 2B,C). The photo of aqueous solutions of CDs-CO2 under the lamp of UV light at 365 nm wavelength shows blue emission which is similar to CDs (Fig. 2A and B). As beet contains a lot of sugars, we tested whether baked sugars with fluorescence can also be enhanced by CO2. However, we did not find that the CO2 increased fluorescence of baked sucrose, fructose or glucose (Fig. 2C). The data suggest that the CDs enhancement is likely specific to CDs, but not to chemical component of beet, which indicates the potential nanoparticle associated effect. The unexpected results also suggest that the potent non-sugar related compounds may involve in the CDs-CO2 association or interplays even sugar may be involved in the CDs binding to negatively charged phopshor- groups as we described previously (Xie et al., 2019).

Considering cluster formation of CD-CO2, this observation suggests that carbon dots’ aggregation can affect their ability to luminesce shown in other studies (Das et al., 2020). One of the explanations might be the phenomenon of Aggregation Induced Emission (AIE). According to this concept, emission intensity can increase due to changes in rotation and vibration within molecules or specific orbital overlaps between molecules (Zhang et al., 2020). For example, one research demonstrated that external state changes of blue-emitting hydrophobic carbon dots induced their aggregation restricting intramolecular motions and causing red-coloured emission (Yang et al., 2019). In another study, phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P–CDs) emission was switched from blue to orange-yellow due to a higher concentration (Li et al., 2017).

The nanoparticles of CDs showed a certain degree of nanozyme activity. The phosphatase-like activity of beet CDs was indicated in previous studies (Xie et al., 2019) and in this study, their mix with carbon dioxide was examined for testing the effect of CO2 on CDs nanozyme activity. Alkaline phosphatase substrate NBT/BCIP was used as a substrate at different pH values, such as 4.7, 7, 9.9, and 13. Given that a blue-coloured product forms due to sufficient phosphatase activity, the absence of colour changes points to the dysfunction of phosphatase-like enzyme activity. We found that CO2 negatively affects the phosphatase activity of beet CDs. At pH 13 with NBT/BCIP as a substrate, the CDs bond with CO2 from both two different sources (CO2 dissolved in water and CO2 produced from the neutralization reaction of NaHCO3 with HCl had a lower enzymatic activity compared to beet CDs ( Fig. 3A). The p values were 0.001692, and 0.00091 respectively by student t-test (Fig. 3A). The source of CO2 from aqueously dissolved CO2 had a similar effect compared to CO2 produced from the reaction of NaHCO3 with HCl. At a lower pH, such as slightly acidic (pH 4.7), neutral (pH 7) and slightly basic (pH 9.92), CDs had low activity at the range of 30%, which is relative to activity of beet CDs at pH 13 as 100% (Fig. 3A). Thus, CDs association with CO2 can affect their phosphatase-like activity. Moreover, the enzyme kinetics of the phosphatase activity driven by CDs-CO2 showed the significant difference from that of CDs alone which shows the decreased Vmax (Fig. 3B). Thus, our data suggest that the CO2 can affect nanozyme characteristics of beet CDs.

As phosphatase may destroy the phosphor-lipid by removing the phosphor groups on the lipids, we further analyzed the potential of CDs-CO2 effect on lipids to simulate the membrane targeting for biological functions. We used BODIPY FL C5-ceramide (Thermo Fisher) as a membrane lipid to test the binding of CDs-CO2 with lipids. To our surprise, the complex is very even in size at 300–500 nm and forms the micelle morphology (Fig. 3C). Thus, our data suggest the potential biological function of the CDs-CO2 in targeting membrane lipids, given the additional evidence from our recent reports (Nurtay et al., 2023).

Based on the above characteristics of CDs-CO2 with lipids which simulate biological membranes to induce cell death, we further tested the functions in bacteria. In previous studies, it was found that CDs have antibacterial properties against both Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative E. coli (Das et al., 2020). Then, we tested CDs-CO2 for antimicrobial properties against Gram-negative E.coli DH5 alpha strain. Beet CD showed high antimicrobial activity. However, the addition of CO2 significantly decreased this property of CDs ( Fig. 4A). The type of the source of CO2 did not show the difference, as both aqueous solution with CO2 and CO2 produced from the reaction of HCl acid and NaHCO3 base. Starting from 40 µl (concentration of CD is 0.05 mg/mL) of CDs-CO2 in which CO2 was obtained from CO2 water, significantly decreased antimicrobial activity compared to beet CDs alone (Fig. 4A). However, at the concentration of CD at 0.05 mg/mL, there was no significant difference between CDs and CDs-CO2 in which CO2 was obtained from NaHCO3-HCl. Thus, our data suggest that CO2 induced bonding, and aggregation may reduce CDs function, which may link to its toxicity in bacteria by targeting membranes, associated with the phosphatase activity mediated modulation of membrane lipids, based on our recent evidence by SEM (Nurtay et al., 2023).

We and others have shown the CDs also have toxicity to cancer cells and normal cells with nanozyme activity (Xie et al., 2017a, 2017b; Fan et al., 2022). To test the effect of CO2 induced aggregation of CDs toxicity to cells, we applied normal lung cells MRC5 and lung cancer A549 cells and found the effect differs depending on the cell lines. In detail, CO2 significantly decreased the cytotoxicity of beet CDs against A549 cells, while not in normal MRC5 cells (Fig. 4B). Moreover, the viability of cells varied with the concentration of CDs as well. A549 cells treated with 0.075 mg/mL of CDs had low viability but the addition of CO2 significantly increased viability (student t-test, P = 0.0026), while the effect was not significant in lower concentrations such as 0.05 mg/mL (student t-test, P = 0.1482). For both normal cells of MRC5 and cancer cells A549 cells, the CO2 alone did not affect the cell viability. The data suggest the CDs-CO2 effect is through CDs aggression or interaction because CO2 alone did not show any inhibition of cell viability in both normal and cancer lung cells. The unexpected results suggest that the normal cells are not sensitive to the CO2-CDs interplay reduced cytotoxicity of CDs. Thus, cancer cells will be more prone to drug resistance to nanomedicine such as CDs, other nanoparticle drugs or delivery carriers.

Next, we applied confocal microscopy to test whether the bioimaging obtained higher quality related to enhanced fluorescence of CDs by CO2. We found confocal microscopy images of A549 cells showed more fluorescence in cells, compared to CDs alone (Fig. 4C). Our data suggest the enhanced bioimaging effect of CDs in cells, which can be applied for imaging of anticancer drugs in vitro or in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential mechanisms of the association of CDs-CO2
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the potential mechanism of aggregation and induced fluorescence, we analyzed the effect of CO2 on charge of beet CDs. We found the positive charged CDs migrate to the negative electrode in electrophoresis by agarose gel. CDs-CO2 showed the delayed tail of the complex band ( Fig. 5A). Given that the CDs have positive charge, while CO2 in solution will be HCO3 - negatively charged, the charge-related binding and neutralization may play a role, at least in part, in the binding and aggregation of CDs (Fig. 5B).

We investigated the effect of carbon dioxide induced CDs aggregation and their biological effect, including photoluminescence, enzymatic activity, biocompatibility, bioimaging, antibacterial functioning and potent charge mediated aggression mechanisms. This study focused on the fluorescence spectrum changes and suggested that CDs may be a new option to develop biosensors of CO2 with the potential to enhance bioimaging properties in cancer cells.

Previous studies showed that CO2 absorbed by microwave synthesized CDs under the light hydrolyzes into methanol and oxidizes water to oxygen (Wang et al., 2020). Bactericidal and antitumor properties of beet derived CDs used in this research can also be related to the production of such toxic by-products. Regarding enzymatic activity, addition of another supporting material may possibly enhance it. Formate dehydrogenase, natural CO2 reductase, immobilized on carbon nanotubes successfully reduced CO2 to formate (Badiani et al., 2022). However, our study provided the biochemical approach of mitigating carbon dioxide by nanotechnology using CDs, and applied the charge attraction-based interplay to biological systems, rather than using chemical reactions approach. Our study broadened the conceptual knowledge of CO2 in nanomedicine or antibiotics resistance, especially in anticancer, which can be applied to clinical trials. Nanotechnology has been applied for overcoming drug resistance by escaping ATP-binding cassette transporter proteins mediated drug efflux but there is also potent drug resistance by carbon nanoparticles. For example, CD itself may induce PARP inhibitor resistance at low dose, while CO2-CDs association may reverse the drug resistance (Fan et al., 2022). The dose effect would be also validated when broadening the application especially in drug delivery (Fan et al., 2022). In addition, the CO2 enhanced CDs fluorescence may be applied to bioimaging of anticancer or antibiotics in clinical application and drug delivery. This conceptual application using CO2 as a mediator in bioimaging and drug delivery would be a novel approach even if the conditions need to be optimized.

For limitations, our studies only provided in vitro analysis of the conceptual findings. Further experiments to address the current study applications would include in vivo animal research as tumor microenvironment including extracellular matrix plays essential roles in cell signaling and drug efficacy in different tissues (Xie et al., 2016). For example, the underlying mechanisms of fluorescence emission should be explored in more detail and antitumor effects should be studied for identifying signaling pathways that disrupt the CDs toxicity. Moreover, the phosphatase activity of CDs-CO2 could be applied for nanozyme-based bioimaging or therapy in the future. For further improvement, it is suggested to test the CO2 conjugated CDs by coupling them together with another artificially derived nanoparticles or natural nanozyme for biomedical applications (Benassi et al., 2021). Moreover, the mechanism underlying the decreased antitumor and antimicrobial functions of CDs by CO2 should be studied as well. Because cancer patients who are undergoing treatment would face large-scale increased CO2 and carbon particles in the near future due to global carbon emission, wildfire, and environmental pollution, studies on the mechanism of carbon on anticancer therapy would be much significant for “One Health”. It would be the same for antibiotics resistance in microbe targeting as we found CO2 significantly blocked the antibacterial effect of CDs, simulating the antibiotic resistance as CDs have antibacterial function (Nurtay et al., 2023). Thus, to prevent the next pandemic after COVID-19, in-depth understanding and studies on carbon emission effects on microbes would be highly needed based on lessons we learned from COVID-19 (Sun et al., 2021). Even though the global warming effect on pandemics is in debate (Zang et al., 2021), carbon emission is obvious and will affect the environment and global health, especially for cancer patients and undergoing evolution of viruses and microbes.

For environmental protection, CO2-CDs association may be a novel conceptual tool for reducing the environmental CO2 over emission. CDs have been applied for reducing pollutants by degradation.However, CO2 reduction application is limited. Our study provided potential neutralization of CO2 in environmental emission in wastewater, mist and fog and other forms of CO2 or HCO3 -. Our study provided a potent mechanism by static charge attractions between HCO3 - and positive charged CDs. This is a novel approach in environmental application using CDs.

CO2 enhanced the aggregation of CDs derived from beet, which is consistent with the CDs characteristics of increased fluorescence intensity, decreased nanozyme activity, decreased toxicity to bacteria and cancer cells but enhanced fluorescence in cells which can be applied for bioimaging or CO2 sensing. Thus, CDs may be applied as a remedy of high emission of CO2 which is of significance for environmental protection, drug resistance overcoming in drug delivery of anticancer agents and antibiotics. The investigation supported the hypothetical potent interplay of CDs-CO2 by surface charge attractions which disrupted the ecotoxicity, and broadened our knowledge of mitigating carbon dioxide by nanotechnology and biophysical interactions, in addition to the currently applied chemical reactions approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
All authors agreed on the publication.
Nazarbayev University FDCRGP grant (grant NO. 11022021FD2920 and 110119FD4531) to Yingqiu Xie; AUA-UAEU grant (12R118) to Amr Amin and Yingqiu Xie.

Mirza Omar: Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Omarova Zhuldyz: Formal analysis, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Amin Amr: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Fan Haiyan: Methodology, Resources. Nazarbek Guldan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization. Myrzagali Sandugash: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Sergazina Akbota: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization. Xie Yingqiu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Zhou Weizhi: Writing – review & editing. Shaimoldina Ainur: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Amr Amin reports statistical analysis, travel, and writing assistance were provided by United Arab Emirates University.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Nazarbayev University FDCRGP grant (11022021FD2920), to Yingqiu Xie; AUA-UAEU grant (12R118) to Amr Amin and Yingqiu Xie. We would like to thank Milana Kondratyeva, Aiganym Aitenova for replicating experiment.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Arcudi et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design, synthesis, and functionalization strategies of tailored carbon nanodots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Arcudi F., Đorđević L., Prato M. Design, Synthesis, and Functionalization Strategies of Tailored Carbon Nanodots. Acc Chem Res. 2019 Aug 20;52(8):2070–2079. doi: 10.1021/acs.accounts.9b00249. Epub 2019 Jul 23. PMID: 31335113.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Azuma et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of low-level inhalation exposure to carbon dioxide in indoor environments: a short review on human health and psychomotor performance
